LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT THE LAMB & FRISCHER LAW FIRM LLC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION AS TO THE SECOND, THIRD AND FOURTH CAUSES OF ACTION FOR CONVERSION, CONSTRUCTIVE TRUST AND FRAUD IN THE COMPLAINT FILED BY PLAINTIFF RUSSELL STANALAND IS DENIED AS TO ALL THREE CAUSES OF ACTION. THE MOTION IS DENIED AS MOOT AS TO THE CONVERSION AND CONSTRUCTIVE TRUST CLAIMS BECAUSE MR. STANALAND DISMISSED THOSE CLAIMS. THE MOTION IS DENIED AS TO THE FRAUD CLAIM BECAUSE L&F FAILED TO MAINTAIN ITS SUMMARY MOTION BURDEN OF PRODUCTION. MR. STANALAND'S FRAUD THEORY IS THAT ONCE L&F LEARNED THAT THE SETTLEMENT OFFERS WERE NOT MEMORIALIZED, WHICH WAS AFTER THE PARTIES ENTERED INTO THEIR AGREEMENT, L&F CONCEALED THE FACT THAT L&F INTENDED TO ASSERT THAT THE AGREEMENT WAS INVALID WHEN IT RECEIVED THE SETTLEMENT FUNDS. (COMPLAINT 3:10-5:16; 7:1-24.) L&F'S FACTS 44-56 FAIL TO SHOW THAT MR. STANALAND'S FRAUD THEORY CANNOT BE ESTABLISHED. COUNSEL FOR PLAINTIFF TO SUBMIT A PROPOSED ORDER THAT IS VERBATIM WITH THE COURT'S RULING AND IN COMPLIANCE WITH CRC 3.1312. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN; CLERK: W. TRUPEK; REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO CSR#8600, EMAIL: TORREANOM@AOL.COM; (302/HEK)

Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Plaintiff Russell Stanaland Dba Stanaland Associates For Judgment On The Pleadings; Request For Judicial Notice
Off Calendar Jun-11-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Eric McFarland, Esq. 415-450-0295 and Russell Rowan, Esq. 415-448-5000 of Thompson R. Welch for Plaintiff.
Richard Frischer, Esq. and Bryan Lamb 415-997-9529 of Lamb & Fischer for Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PLAINTIFF RUSSELL STANALAND DOING BUSINESS AS STANALAND'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings; Request For Judicial Notice.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Plaintiff and Cross-defendant Stanaland's motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted without leave to amend as to the fraudulent inducement and negligent misrepresentation causes of action. The litigation privilege bars the claims. (See Civ. Code, 47(b).) The alleged communications were in connection with identifying competent counsel that could represent the Neumanns in the personal injury action. (Cross-Complaint, 11-16.) In Olsen v. Harbison (2010) 191 Cal.App.4th 325, the court held on closely similar facts that statements that "'accompanied the negotiation of terms'" between co-counsel were privileged. (Olsen, 191 Cal.App.4th at 333.) The court explained:

As we have repeatedly noted, defendant [co-counsel] made his comments when discussing the possibility of becoming associated in on the Klawitter case. This conversation was part of plaintiff's efforts to bring in experienced counsel to assist on the case, take responsibility for the actual trial, and help Klawitter obtain a verdict or settlement. Had there been no litigation, these comments would never have been made. Remarks made in a setting like this can only be deemed connected to the litigation and subject to the litigation privilege. (see word minutes for full details)(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

Plaintiff to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
Off Calendar Jun-11-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Eric McFarland, Esq. 415-450-0295 and Russell Rowan, Esq. 415-448-5000 of Thompson R. Welch for Plaintiff.
Richard Frischer, Esq. and Bryan Lamb 415-997-9529 of Lamb & Fischer for Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT/CROSS-COMPLAINANT THE LAMB & FRISCHER LAW FIRM's Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.The Lamb & Frischer Law Firm, LLP's motion for judgment on the pleadings on the fourth cause of action for fraud and deceit is granted without leave to amend.

As the court previously noted, Plaintiff's theory of recovery on the fraud claim is that once Defendant learned that the settlement offers were not memorialized, which was after the parties entered into their agreement, Defendant concealed the fact that Defendant intended to assert that the agreement was invalid when it received the settlement funds. (Complaint, 3:10-5:16; 7:1-24; Order Denying Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication entered 12/19/18.)

Plaintiff fails to plead a promissory fraud, deceit, or concealment claim.

"'Promissory fraud' is a subspecies of the action for fraud and deceit. A promise to do something necessarily implies the intention to perform; hence, where a promise is made without such intention, there is an implied misrepresentation of fact that may be actionable fraud. [Citations.] An action for promissory fraud may lie where a defendant fraudulently induces the plaintiff to enter into a contract." (Lazar v. Superior Court (1996) 12 Cal.4th 631, 638.)

Here, Plaintiff does not allege that Defendant entered into the fee agreement with no intent to perform. In fact, the allegations of the complaint show that Defendant was performing. Paragraph 7 alleges that the parties entered into the agreement on 3/28/16 and Paragraphs 12 and 27 show that defendant performed the contract and brokered a settlement of the personal injury action. Paragraph 27 specifically alleges that "L&F continued to perform services under the Fee Split Agreement . . . ."

Plaintiff does not allege concealment. (See Hoffman v. 162 North Wolfe LLC (2014) 228 Cal.App.4th 1178, 1185-1186 ["As with all fraud claims, the necessary elements of a concealment/suppression claim consist of '(1) misrepresentation (false representation, concealment, or nondisclosure); (2) knowledge of falsity (scienter); (3) intent to defraud (i.e., to induce reliance); (4) justifiable reliance; and (5) resulting damage.'"].

There is no concealment where a party honestly enters into a contract, begins to perform, and then along the way determines that it will breach the contract. There is no tort liability for concealing an intention to breach a contract under such circumstances.

Applied Equipment is instructive. "But the law generally does not distinguish between good and bad motives for breaching a contract. '[I]n traditional contract law, the motive of the breaching party generally has no bearing on the scope of damages that the injured party may recover for the breach of the implied covenant [of good faith and fair dealing]; the remedies are limited to contract damages.'" (Applied Equip. Corp. v. Litton Saudi Arabia Ltd. (1994) 7 Cal.4th 503, 516.)

Since Plaintiff fails to show that there is a reasonable possibility to cure the defective claim (and appears to be relying on an inapplicable promissory fraud theory), Defendant's motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted without leave to amend. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Off Calendar Dec-10-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Eric McFarland, Esq., Thompson & Welch, Soroko & Gilbert LLP, Telephone 415-480-0285, appearing for Plaintiff Russell Stanaland;
Marri A. Baldwin, Esq., Rogers Joseph O'Donnell,Telephone 415-365-5311, appearing for Defendant The Lamb & Frischer Law Firm, LLP;
Richard L. Frischer, Esq., Lamb & Frischer, Telephone 415-997-9272, appearing for Defendant The Lamb & Frischer Law Firm, LLP;

Ruling - A R G U E D. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant The Lamb & Frischer Law Firm LLC's motion for summary adjudication as to the second, third and fourth causes of action for conversion, constructive trust and fraud in the complaint filed by plaintiff Russell Stanaland is denied as to all three causes of action. The motion is denied as moot as to the conversion and constructive trust claims because Mr. Stanaland dismissed those claims. The motion is denied as to the fraud claim because L&F failed to maintain its summary motion burden of production. Mr. Stanaland's fraud theory is that once L&F learned that the settlement offers were not memorialized, which was after the parties entered into their agreement, L&F concealed the fact that L&F intended to assert that the agreement was invalid when it received the settlement funds. (Complaint 3:10-5:16; 7:1-24.) L&F's facts 44-56 fail to show that Mr. Stanaland's fraud theory cannot be established.

Counsel for Plaintiff to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

HEARING RE: EX PARTE APPLICATION REQUESTING CLARIFICATION OF ORDER.

On the parties' applications regarding the money held in the account at Bank of San Francisco in the name of Lamb & Frischer Law Firm, LLP, all money in that account, including any interest thereon, must remain in that account until either an agreement of the parties or court order regarding the distribution of that money. Lamb & Frischer's assertion of a quantum meruit right to the money in the account rather than claiming the amount set forth in the parties' share agreement creates the possibility that Lamb & Frischer will ultimately receive less than the $500,000 that it contends should now be disbursed to it. Because Lamb & Frischer may ultimately receive less than $500,000, it would be inequitable to distribute $500,000 to it now.
